questions.text: I find that emotional events don't leave a long residue; I process and move on.
I find that I can engage with the most difficult aspects of the human condition without it affecting my capacity to function.
I like when the scope of work is something the whole team defines together.
I find that focusing on what I want—rather than what others expect—leads to my best life.
I build checklists for recurring processes so nothing gets skipped.
I feel that the moral claim of another's suffering is something I carry forward even when the moment of encounter has passed.
I tend to carve out my own lane rather than share one.
My nervous system seems to treat emotional stimuli the way other people's systems treat physical pain — with urgency.
I feel uncomfortable starting a project without a clear outline of the steps involved.
I experience the tension before a performance or presentation as an almost intolerable buildup of energy.
I would use my professional influence to open doors for others rather than to advance my own agenda.
I experience the full range of human emotion but at a volume that never becomes distressing.
I think that the most constructive thing I can do during someone's emotional crisis is to remain focused on identifying concrete solutions.
I make decisions and move forward without documenting the rationale behind each one.
I routinely write thoughtful, detailed feedback for others' projects even when no one asks.
I am energized by having the final say.
I find that my reactions to kindness are just as intense as my reactions to cruelty — both hit me hard.
I can tell when someone changes the subject not to avoid discomfort but to protect someone else from it.
I rarely sense when someone is tailoring their message based on who is listening.
I'd rather commit quickly to a direction and learn from the results than test endlessly.
I am energized by helping a group articulate its shared vision to stakeholders.
I feel that any privilege I hold comes with a duty to redistribute it.
I can feel when a social gathering has an invisible guest list that determines who was and was not expected.
I tend not to notice when someone is deliberately making themselves small in a social setting.
I feel that the degree to which suffering functions as a genuine priority in my life is the best test of whether my moral convictions are real.
I'd rather try something immediately and learn from the result than spend time planning it out.
I believe that the moderate volume of my emotional life gives me a certain clarity of perception.
I tend to miss when someone frames bad news inside good news to soften the blow.
I feel that the moral imperative to address suffering grows stronger the more clearly I understand what someone is going through.
I feel that working on my own side projects in the evenings is more rewarding than socializing.
I believe that emotional composure during a crisis is not a luxury but a necessity for clear and fair decision-making.
I tend to seek out roles and projects that will look good on my own record.
I find it rewarding to have the final word in a discussion.
I maintain a clear separation between planning time and execution time.
I am typically the one who helps the group keep deliberating until a tie resolves itself.
I sense when a person is testing the loyalty of others by sharing a piece of gossip and watching where it goes.
